title: Standard iGEM Cell Measurement Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"8cclg","text":"This is a protocol from the 2018 iGEM InterLab study which tests various 'standard' parts from the Registry.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"6ocbt","text":"Help with debugging:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2qb4g","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"utrg","text":"If you have measurements that are off scale (“OVERFLOW”), that data will not be usable. You need to adjust your settings so that the data will be in range and re-run your calibration.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fq30f","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7p0h7","text":"If your Abs600 measurements for your cell colonies are very close to that of your LB with Chloramphenicol, then your cells have probably not been transformed correctly or grown correctly.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ck726","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6d8l6","text":"If your negative and positive control values are very close to each other, that probably means something has gone wrong in your protocol or measurement.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"7blt3","text":"Prior to performing this protocol you should complete the following calibration protocols: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":91}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1sarj","text":"OD600 Inter-equipment Conversion with LUDOX","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":2,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dd7e2","text":"Particle Standard Curve with Microspheres","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e5f0e","text":"Fluorescence Standard Curve with Fluorescein  ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1cbqu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7frrk","text":"Completion of the calibrations will ensure that you understand the measurement process and that you can take the cell measurements under the same conditions.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4ljs5","text":"For all of these cell measurements, you must use the same plates and volumes that you used in your previous calibration protocols. You must also use the same settings (e.g., filters or excitation and emission wavelengths) that you used in your calibration measurements. If you do not use the same plates, volumes, and settings, the measurements will not be valid.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8qekj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bef47","text":"Read through this entire protocol carefully before you start your experiment and","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"448i2","text":"prepare any materials you may need. See the Guidelines tab on this page for debugging help.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b8iil","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5ocqg","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":null,"entityRanges":null,"data":{}},{"key":"6cswt","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":{}},{"key":"eb8au","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"image","mutability":"MUTABLE","data":{"guid":"61571BC0341F11EE9D4302C0B41BC903","height":333,"is_image":true,"is_video":false,"legend":"{\"blocks\":null,\"entityMap\":null}","mime":"image/jpeg","original_name":"Cell measurement protocol workflow.jpg","placeholder":"","source":"https://content.protocols.io/bfs94df6.jpg","width":698}}}}

steps: Transform ​Escherichia coli​ DH5α with these following plasmids (all in pSB1C3) %s %s Help Debugging Your Transformations: We STRONGLY recommend that you use the iGEM protocol to create your competent cells Once you have created your competent cells, we STRONGLY recommend that you measure the competency of your cells using the Competent Cell Test Kit Finally, we strongly recommend that you closely follow the iGEM protocols for resuspending DNA from the kit plates and performing the transformation Year after year, we have found that most teams are highly successful when they follow these protocols, even if alternative protocols are used within your lab. If you are having trouble transforming your test devices, please try the protocols above

Pick 2 colonies from each of the transformation plates and inoculate in 5-10 mL LB medium + Chloramphenicol. Grow the cells overnight (16-18 hours) at 37 °C and 220 rpm. Liquid LB medium Contributed by users Chloramphenicol (25 mg/ml in EtOH) Contributed by users
Make a 1:10 dilution of each overnight culture in LB with Chloramphenicol (0.5 mL of culture into 4.5 mL of LB with Chloramphenicol)
Measure the Abs​ 600 ​ of these 1:10 diluted cultures
Record the data in your notebook

Dilute the cultures further to a target Abs 6​00 of 0.02 in a final volume of ​12 mL LB medium with Chloramphenicol in a 50 mL falcon tube Falcon tube should be amber, or covered with foil to block light

Take 500 μL samples of the diluted cultures at 0 hours into 1.5 mL eppendorf tubes, prior to incubation. At each time point between 0 hours and 6 hours, you will take a sample from each of the 8 devices, two colonies per device, for a total of 16 eppendorf tubes with 500 μL samples per time point, 32 samples total. Place the samples on ice.
Incubate the remainder of the cultures at 37 °C and 220 rpm for 6 hours
Take 500 μL samples of the cultures at 6 hours of incubation into 1.5 mL eppendorftubes. Place samples on ice.

At the end of the experiment, you should have two plates to read. You will have one plate for each time point: 0 and 6 hours. On each plate you will read both fluorescence and absorbance. Samples should be laid out according to the plate diagram below. Plate layout for cell measurement calibration protocol

Pipette 100 μL of each sample into each well. From 500 μL samples in a 1.5 mL eppendorf tube: 4 replicate samples of colony #1 should be pipetted into wells in rows A, B, C and D Replicate samples of colony #2 should be pipetted into wells in rows E, F, G and H

Be sure to include 8 control wells containing 100 μL each of only LB with Chloramphenicol on each plate in column 9, as shown in the diagram. Set the instrument settings as those that gave the best results in your calibration curves (no measurements off scale). If necessary you can test more than one of the previously calibrated settings to get the best data (no measurements off scale). Instrument temperature should be set to room temperature (approximately 20-25 C) if your instrument has variable temperature settings
You have now completed this calibration protocol
creator: Paul Rutten
